title: 第 44 節
en: Next follows the definition of dispassion (Vair&gya), as the consciousness of having overcome (the world) on the part of one who has no longer any desire for any objects whatsoever, whether visible or revealed (I, 15).

Here visible A(Dnshtfa) stands for perceptible or sensuous objects, while Anusr&vika may be translated by revealed, as it is derived from Anusrava, and this is identical with $ruti or Veda. Perhaps Anusrava is more general than DISPASSION, VAIRAGYA. 339 Veda, including all that has been handed down, such as the stories about the happiness of the gods in paradise (Devaloka), &c. The consciousness oi* having subdued or overcome all such desires and being no longer the slave of them, that, we are told, is Vairagya or dispassionateness, and that is the highest point which the student of Yogaphilosophy hopes to reach.

It is interesting to see how deeply this idea of Vairagya or dispassionateness must have entered into the daily life of the Hindus. It- is constantly mentioned as the highest excellence not for ascetics only, but for everybody. It sometimes does not mean much more than what we mean by the even and subdued temper of the true gentleman, but it signifies also the highest unworldliness and a complete surrender of all selfish desires. A very good description of what Vair&gya is or ought to be is preserved to us in the hundred verses ascribed to Bhartrihari (650 A.D.), which are preceded by two other centuries of verses, one on worldly wisdom and the other on love. Many of these verses occur again and again in other works, and it is very doubtful whether Bhartrihari was really the original author of them all, or whether he only collected them as SubMshitas l. Anyhow they show how the philosophy of Vairagya had leavened the popular mind of India at that distant time, nor has it ceased to do so to the present day. It was perhaps bold, after BhartHhari, to undertake a similar collection of verses on the same subject. But as the Vairagya-sataka of (?ain&&arya seems in more recent times to have acquired considerable popularity in India, a few extracts from it may serve to show that the old teaching of Patatf $ali and Bhartrihari has not yet been forgotten in their native country.

' Death follows man like a shadow, and pursues him like an enemy ; perform, therefore, good deeds, so that you may reap a blessing hereafter/ ' Frequent enjoyment of earthly prosperity has led to your sufferings. Pity it is that you have not tried the " Know Yourself."'
1 His work is actually called Subhashita-trisati, see Report of Sanskrit and Tamil MSS., 1896-97, by Seshagiri Sastri, p. 7.

' Live in the world but be not of it, is the precept taught by our old Rishis, and it is the only means of liberating yourself from the werld/ ' The body is perishable and transitory, while the Self is imperishable and everlasting ; it is connected with the body only by the link of Karman ; it should not be subservient to it/ ' If, through sheer negligence, you do nothing good for your fellow creatures, you will be your own enemy, and become a victim to the miseries of this world/ ' Better to do less good, with purity of heart, than to do more with jealousy, pride, malice, 'or fraud. Little, but good and loving work, is always valuable, like a pure gem, the essence of a drug, or pithy advice/ 'If you are unable to subject yourself physically to penances, to undergo austerities, and engage in deep contemplation, the proper course to liberate your soul from the hard fetters of Karman would be to keep the passions of your heart under control, to check your desires, to carry out your secular affairs with calmness, to devote yourself to the worship of God, and to realise in yourself the tc Permanent Truth/' bearing in mind the transitory nature of the universe/ c To control your mind, speech, and body, does not mean to be thoughtless, silent or inactive, like beasts or trees ; but, instead of thinking what is evil, speaking untruth, and doing harm to others, mind, speech, and body should be applied to good thoughts, good words, and good deeds/ Dispassionateness, as here taught for practical purposes chiefly, reaches its highest point in the eyes of the Yogaphilosopher, when a man, after he has attained to the knowledge of Purusha, has freed himself entirely from all desire for the three Gums (or their products). This is at least what Pata//c/ali says in a somewhat obscure Sutra (I, n)\ This Sfttra seems intended to describe the highest state within reach of the true Vair&gin, involving indifference not only to visible and revealed objects, but likewise towards the GuTias, that is^ if I am not mistaken, the 1 6-arbe, Grundriss, p. 49.

MEDITATION WITH OE WITHOUT AN OBJECT. 341 twenty-four Tattvas, here called Gimas1, because determined by them. The knowledge of the Purusha implies the distinction between what is Purusha, the Self, and what is not, and therefore also between Purusha and the GuTias of Prakriti. Vigr^ana-Bhikshu explains it by Atm&natmavivekas&kshatkarat, i.e. from realising the difference between what is Self and what is not Self, and not as a possessive compound : the sense, however, remaining much the same. It is curious that Rajendralal Mitra should have rendered Purushakhy&teA by ' conducive to a knowledge of God/ From a purely philosophical point of view Purusha may be translated by God, but such a translation would be misleading here, particularly as the Sutra 23, on the devotion to the Lord, follows so soon after. It would have been better also to translate ' arising from/ than c conducive to/ Meditation With or Without an Object.

Pata/?^ali next proceeds (I, 17) to explain an important distinction between the two kinds of meditative absorption (SamMhi), which he calls Sampras/Sat^ and Asamprag^&ta. This seems to mean that there is one kind of meditation when our thoughts are directed and fixed on a definite object, and another when there is no definite object of meditation left. Here the spirit of minute distinction shows itself once more, for though these two kinds of meditation may well be kept apart, and the former be considered as preliminary to the latter, the numerous subdivisions of each hardly deserve our notice. We are told that what is called conscious meditation may have for its object either one or the other of the twenty-four Tattvas or the Isvara, looked upon as one of the Purushas. The twenty-four 1 These Gu^as are more fully described in II, 19, where we read that the four Gunas or Gunaparvam are meant for (i) Visesha, i. e. the gross elements and the organs; (a) Avisesha, i.e. the subtle elements and the mind ; (3) the Liwgamatra, i.e. Buddhi ; (4) the Alimga, i.e. Prakn'ti as Avyakta. In the commentary to I, 45, the same classes of Gunas are described as Aliwga, a name of Pradhana, "Visishfeiliwga, the gross elements (Bhutani); Avisishfoliwga, the subtle essences and the senses; Liwgamatra, i.e. Buddhi, and Alimga, that is, the Pradhana.

Tattvas are called unconscious, the twenty-fifth or Purusna is conscious. When meditation (Bhavana) has something definite for its object it is called not only Prapwata, known, or, as referred to the subject, knowing, but also Savi^a, literally with a seed, which I am inclined to take in the sense of having some seed on which it can fix, and from which it can develop. The Asampragwata-samadhi, or meditation without a known object, is called Avi^/a, not having a seed from which to spring or to expand. Native commentators, however, take a different view.

Those who in their Samadhi do not go beyond the twenty- four Tattvas, without seeing the twenty-fifth, the Purusha, but at all events identify themselves no longer with the body, are called Videhas, bodyless ; others who do not see the Purusha yet, but only existence, are called Prakritilayas, absorbed in Prakriti. , This again is not quite clear to me, but it is hardly necessary that we should enter into all the intricate subdivisions of the two kinds of meditation, such as SavitarkS,, argumentative, Savi&ara, deliberative, Sananda, joyous, and Sasmit& *, with false conceit. They may become important in a more minute study of the Yoga, but they can hardly be of interest to speculative philosophers except so far as they furnish another proof of a long continued study of the Toga-philosophy in India before the actual composition of the Sutras.

The Asampra<7/?ata-sam&dhi, or meditation without a known object, or, it may be, unconscious meditation, is explained as being preceded by a repetition of negative perception, and as the end of all previous impressions.

This Sutra has been differently explained by different European and native commentators. It may mean that there is a residue of previous impressions, or that there is not. The Samskaras, which I have rendered by previous impressions, are everything that has given to the mind its 1 Asmita is different from Ahawkara, and means the misconception that I am (Asmi) what I am nott such as Praknti, Buddhi, Ahamkara, Manas, &c.

LSVABA ONCE MORE. 343 peculiar character, its flavour, so to say3 or its general disposition, 'Quo semel est imbuta recena servabit odorem Testa din/ It may be intended that these Samsk&ras are either all wiped out, or that there is but a small residue of them, manifested in the final act of the stopping all functions of the mind.

In summing up what has been said about the different kinds of Samadhi, Patan^ali says (I, 19) once more that in the case of the Videhas and Prakritilayas (as explained before, p. 342) the object or, if you like, the cause of SamMhi is the real world (Bhava), but that for other Yogins there are preliminary conditions or steps to Samadhi, namely, faith, energy, memory, concentration, and knowledge succeeding each other. Every one of these SamMhis is again carefully defined, and some more helps are mentioned in the next Sutra (I, 21), where we read that Sam&dhi may be said to be near or within reach when the zeal or the will is strong. These strong-willed or determined aspirants are again divided (I, 22) according as the means employed by them are mild, moderate, or excessive. Thus we get nine classes of Togins, those who employ mild means, with rnild, with moderate, or with excessive zeal ; those who employ moderate means, with mild, with moderate, or with excessive zeal; and those who employ excessive means with mild, with moderate, or with excessive zeal.

Such divisions and subdivisions which fully justify the name of S&mkhya, enumeration, make both the Samkhyaand Yoga-philosophies extremely tedious, and I shall in future dispense with them, though they may contain now and then some interesting observations.
fsvara Once More.

After an enumeration of all these means of Yoga to be employed by the student, follows at last the famous Sfttra I, 23, which has always been supposed to contain, in answer to Kapila, the proof of the existence of a Deity, and which I translated before by ' Devotion to the Lord/ The corn- 344 INDIAN PHILOSOPHY.

mentator calls it simply an easy expedient, an alternative. Nor is it right, with Kajendralal Mitra, to translate this Sutra at once by c Devotion to God.' Isvara, as we saw, is not God in the sense in which Brahma might be called so. He is a God, the highest God, but always one of many Punishas ; and though he was looked upon as holy (I, 25; and omniscient, he never seems to have risen to the rank of a Creator, for which there is really no room in the Samkhya system. Though it is true, no doubt, that the orthodox Yogins derived great comfort from this Sutra as shielding Patawgrali against the charge of atheism, it would be impossible to look upon it as a real proof in support of the existence of God, or as more than a somewhat forced confession of faith.
Other Means of Obtaining1 Samadhi.

The benefits arising from this devotion to the Lord are not essentially different from those that are to be obtained from other Upayas or means of attaining Samadhi, as may be seen from Sutras I, 29 to I, 33 translated before. Nor is this devotion even the last or the highest Upaya, for Pata??<7ali goes on immediately after to mention other means equally conducive to concentrated meditation or absorption in the thought of one object. Expedients, such as the expulsion and retention of the breath, follow next, the socalled Prattayamas, which we can well believe, may have been really useful as contrivances to draw away the thoughts from ah1 subjects except the one chosen for meditation, generally one of the Tattvas. But this opens far too large a subject for our purpose in this place. We approach here to the pathological portion of the Yoga, the so-called Hatf&a or Kriy&-yoga, a subject certainly far more important than has generally been supposed, but a subject for students of pathology rather than of philosophy, unless, ajs is now the fashion, we include the so-called physico -psychological experiments under the name of philosophy. One thing may certainly be claimed for our Sutras; they are honest in their statements as to the discipline that can be applied to the mind through the body, and even if they could be proved to have been mistaken in their observations, their OTHER MEANS OF OBTAINING SAMADHI. 345 illusions do not seem to me to have been mere frauds, at least in the days of Pata/?#ali. though it is far from my purpose to undertake a defence of all the doings and sayings of modern Yogins or Mahatmans.

Next to the moderation or restraint of the "breathing, follow descriptions of how the mind, by being directed to the tip of the nose, cognises a heavenly odour, and the same with all the other senses, which therefore are supposed to have no longer any inclination towards outward objects, having everything they want in themselves. We are next told of the perception of an inward luminous and blessed state, which produces a steadiness and contentedness of the mind when directed towards objects which no longer appeal to the passions (I, 37). No wonder that even objects seen in dreams or in sleep are supposed to answer the same purpose, that is, to fix the attention. In fact any object may be chosen for steady meditation, such as the moon without, or our heart within, provided always that these objects do not appeal to our passions.

All these are means towards an end, and there can be no doubt that they have proved efficacious ; only, as so often happens^ the means have evidently encroached in this case also, on the aims, and to such an extent that Yoga has often been understood to consist in these outward efforts rather than in that concentration of thought which they were meant to produce, and which was to lead on to Kaivalya or spiritual separateness and freedom. This true Yoga is often distinguished as Ka#a-yoga or royal Yoga from the other called Kriya-yoga or working Yoga, which is sometimes called Ha£Aa-yoga, though it is not clear why. Though some of these bodily exercises are represented as serving as a kind of staircase on which the mind ascends step by step, we are told at other times that any step may be useful, and that some may be skipped or taken for passed.

Now, if we ask what is the result of all this, we are told in Sfttra 41 that a man who has put an end to all the motions and emotions of his mind, obtains with regard to all objects of his senses conformation grounded in them (sic), or steadiness and consubstantiation, the idea being that the mind is actually modified or changed by the INDIAN PHILOSOPHY.

objects perceived (I, 41). As a crystal, when placed near a red flower, becomes really red to our eyes, in the same way the mind is supposed to become tinged by the objects perceived. This impression remains true as grounded in the object, and our mind should always be centred on one object of meditation.

Having mentioned in a former Sfttra that Samadhi (here called Samapatti) may be either Savitarka or Savi^ara, he now explains (I, 42) that when meditation is mixed with uncertainties as to word, meaning, or knowledge, it is called Savitarka. Thus, supposing that our meditation was centred on a cow, the question would be whether we should meditate on the sound cow, Sk. Go, or on the meaning of it (Begriff), that is the genus cow, or the idea or picture (Vorstellung) conveyed by it. Such a meditation would be called Savitarka. Its opposite is NirvitarkU when all memory vanishes and the meaning alone, without any form, remains, or, as the commentator puts it, though, not much more clearly, when the knowing mind (Pragma), tinged with the form of its object, forgets its own subjective form of knowing, and becomes, as it were, one in form with the object.

After Samadhi, both Savitarka and Nirvitark&, has been described, the next division is into Savi&ara and Nirvi/tara. They are defined as having reference to subtle objects (I, 44), that is, to the Tanmatras, essences, and the senses, and thus we learn that the former, the Savitarka Samadhi, had to deal with material objects only. Subtle objects include Prakriti also, and there is nothing subtle beyond it, for the Purusha is neither subtle nor non-subtle.

If we look upon the Nirvi&ara Samadhi as the highest of the Samadhis, then there would follow on the completion of that meditation contentment or peace of the Self (Atman). Knowledge in this state is called JJitambhara, right or truth-bearing, quite different from the knowledge which is acquired by inference or by revelation. And from this knowledge springs a disposition which overcomes all former dispositions and renders them superfluous.

KAIVALTA, FREEDOM. 347 Sam&dhi This knowledge therefore would seem to be the highest goal of the true Yogin ; but no, there is still something beyond knowledge, and that is what was called before Apra<7/z&t& Sam&dhi, meditation without any object, or pure ecstasy. This restores the Purusha to his own nature, after he has been delivered from all the outside disturbances of life, and particularly from the ignorance that caused him to identify himself for awhile with any of the works of Prakrzti (AsmiiA).
Kaivalya, Freedom.

This short account of what is contained in the first chapter of the Yoga-Sutras contains almost all that can be of interest to European philosophers in the system of Pata;?<?ali, and it is not impossible that it may have originally formed a book complete in itself. It shows us the whole drift of the Yoga in its simplest f orm, beginning with the means of steadying and concentrating the mind on certain things, and more particularly on the twenty- four Tattvas, as taken over from the S&mkhya, and leading on to a description of meditation, no longer restricted to any of the Tattvas, which is tantamount to a meditation which does not dwell on anything that can be offered by an ideal representation of what is called the real world. It is really meditation of each Purusha on himself only, as distinct from all the Tattvas of Prakriti. This is Kaivalya or the highest bliss in the eyes of the true Yogin, and it may well be called the highest achievement of (rMna-yoga, i. e. Yoga carried on by thought or by the will alone. Outward helps, such as the Pr&n&y&ma, the in- and out-breathing, are just alluded to, but that is almost the only allusion to what in later times came to be the most prominent part of the practical or Kriy&-yoga, namely, the postures and other ascetic performances (Yogangas), supposed to prepare the mind for its own higher efforts. The above-mentioned Isvara-praradMna, ' Devotion to the Lord/ is classed here as simply one of the Yog&ngas or accessories of Yoga, together with purification, contentment, penance, and mumbling of prayers (II, 32), showing how little of real INDIAN PHILOSOPHY.
zh: 其次是對無欲（Vairāgya）的定義，即一個人不再對任何事物有任何渴望——無論是可見的或啟示的——而自知已克服（世界）的意識（I, 15）。

這裡的可見之物 A（Dṛśyatva）代表可感知或感性的對象，而 Anusrāvika 可被譯為「啟示的」，因為它源自 Anusrava，而這與 Śruti 或吠陀相同。也許 Anusrava 比「離欲、無執著」(DISPASSION, VAIRAGYA) 更為寬泛。339 吠陀包含了一切流傳下來的內容，例如關於諸神在天堂（提婆洛迦）中幸福的故事等等。克服或戰勝所有此類慾望，並不再是它們奴隸的意識狀態，我們被告知，這就是毗若伽（Vairagya）或離欲的境界，也是瑜伽哲學的修習者希望達到的最高境界。

看到毘若吉耶（Vairagya）或無執著之觀念如何深深地滲透於印度人的日常生活，實在令人感到興趣。這個觀念不斷被提及為最高的卓越品德，不僅適用於苦行者，亦適用於所有人。有時它的涵義不過是我們所謂真正紳士那種沈著而克制的氣質，但它也指最高的超越世俗性以及對一切私利欲望的完全捨棄。一個對於毘若吉耶究竟是何物或應當是何物的極佳描述，保存在歸為跋陀羅笈多（Bhartrihari，650 A.D.）所著的百頌之中，此前另有兩百頌，一部論述世俗智慧，另一部論述愛情。這些頌文中有許多在其他著作中一再出現，因此甚為可疑的是跋陀羅笈多究竟是否真為其全部作品的原著者，抑或他只是將之彙集為「妙頌」（SubMshitas）而已。無論如何，這些頌文表明毘若吉耶的哲學如何在遠古時代就已經酵發了印度的民間思想，並且至今仍未停止發揮此作用。在跋陀羅笈多之後，若要著手進行關於同一主題的類似頌文彙集，或許是大膽之舉。但既然（尚）迦羅耶的《毘若吉耶百頌》（Vairagya-sataka）似乎在近代時期在印度獲得相當的流行，從中擷取幾段摘錄或許足以表明帕坦迦利（Patatf $ali）與跋陀羅笈多的古老教訓在其故國仍未被遺忘。
「死亡如影子般跟隨人類，如敵人般追趕他；因此，你應當行善，以便日後能獲得祝福。」頻繁享受塵世的繁榮已導致你的苦難。可惜的是，你沒有嘗試過「認識你自己」(Know Yourself)。
他的著作實際上稱為《三百箴言》（Subhashita-trisati），見 Seshagiri Sastri 所著《梵文與泰米爾文手稿報告》（Report of Sanskrit and Tamil MSS.），1896-97 年，第 7 頁。

「生活在世界中，但不屬於世界，這是我們古聖仙人（Rishis）所教導的戒律，它是解放你自己脫離世界的唯一途徑。」「身體是易朽的、短暫的，而真我（Self）是不朽的、永恆的；它與身體的連結僅藉由業（Karman）之鏈；它不應受身體支配。」「如果由於純粹的疏忽，你對同類生靈不做任何善事，你將成為自己的敵人，淪為此世苦難的受害者。」「寧可以純淨之心做少許善行，也勝於以嫉妒、驕傲、惡意或欺詐之心做更多善行。少而善、充滿愛心的工作，永遠是有價值的，如同純淨的寶石、藥物的精華，或中肯的勸告。」「如果你無力在身體上進行苦行、承受禁欲，並深入冥想，那麼解放靈魂脫離業（Karman）之堅固枷鎖的適當途徑，就是保持你心中的情慾受控，制約你的欲望，以平靜心境處理你的世俗事務，致身於上帝的崇拜，並在你自身中體現「永恆真理」——銘記宇宙的短暫本質。」「控制你的心、言、行，並非意謂如同野獸或樹木般無思、沉默或不動；相反，心、言、行應當用於善念、善言、善行，而非思惡、言妄、害人。」「無執著性，如此教導主要用於實踐目的，在瑜伽哲學家的眼中達到最高點，當一個人在證得普魯沙（Purusha）的知識後，已完全從對三德（Gunas）或其果報的一切渴望中解脫出來。這至少是帕坦伽利（Patanjali）在某部晦澀難懂的經文中所言（I, II）。」此經文似乎意在描述真正遁世者（Vairāgin）所能達到的最高境界，涉及不僅對可見與顯示對象的冷漠，而且對德性（Gunas）的冷漠——如果我沒有誤解的話——

二十四個本質（tattva），此處稱為基瑪（Gimas），乃因由此等決定之故。對於普魯沙（Purusha）之知識蘊涵著普魯沙（即自我）與非普魯沙之間的區別，因此亦蘊涵著普魯沙與實質（Prakriti）之諸德性（Gunas）之間的區別。毗格涅那—比克蘇（Vigñana-Bhikshu）以「自我與非自我之區別的現證」（Atm&natmavivekas&kshatkarat）來解釋之，即出自實現自我與非自我之間的區別，而非作為所有格複合詞；其意義然則大抵相同。拉真德拉‧拉爾‧米特拉（Rajendralal Mitra）將普魯沙‧克雅特（Purushakhy&teA）譯為「導於對神之知識」，這誠屬奇特。從純粹哲學的觀點而言，普魯沙固然得譯為神，然此等譯法在此則易生誤導，尤其當《經文》第二十三條（論對主宰之虔敬）隨即跟進之時。將「出自」而非「導致」作為譯詞，亦為更佳之選。 **有無對象之冥想**

帕坦迦利接著進行說明（第一卷第17經）兩種冥想吸收（Samadhi）之間的一項重要區分，他稱之為Samprajñata與Asamprajñata。這似乎是指存在著一種冥想，其時我們的思想被導向並固定於一個明確的對象，以及另一種冥想，其中不再留有任何明確的冥想對象。在此處，精細區分的精神再次展現自身，因為雖然這兩種冥想方式確實可以分別保持，且前者可被視為後者的預備階段，但每一種的眾多細分實在不值得我們注意。我們被告知，所謂有意識的冥想可以將以下對象之一作為其對象：二十四個本質原理（Tattvas）之中的任何一個，或伊什瓦羅（Isvara），後者被視為眾多本我（Purushas）之一。二十四個¹ ¹ 這些特質（Gunas）在第二卷第19經中有更詳盡的描述，其中我們讀到四種特質或特質級別（Gunaparvam）分別用於：(i)殊異性（Visesha），即粗大元素和官能；(2)無殊異性（Avisesha），即微細元素和心意；(3)微標記（Lingamatra），即覺慧（Buddhi）；(4)無標記（Alinga），即未顯現的物質原質（Prakrti as Avyakta）。在第一卷第45經的註疏中，這些特質被描述為無標記（Alinga），這是原始根源（Pradhana）的名稱；「有殊異性的標記」，即粗大元素（Bhutani）；「無殊異性的標記」，即微細本質和感官；「微標記」，即覺慧（Buddhi）；以及「無標記」，亦即原始根源（Pradhana）。

諸原質（Tattvas）被稱為無意識的，而第二十五者或補盧沙（Purusna）則是有意識的。當禪定（Bhavana）具有確定的對象時，它不僅被稱為般若多（Prapwata），即已知的，或就主體而言，認知的，而且也被稱為有種（Savi^a），字面上意為「帶有種子」，我傾向於將其理解為具有某種種子，可以固著於其上，並可從中發展。無種三摩地（Asampragwata-samadhi），即沒有已知對象的禪定，被稱為無種（Avi^/a），即沒有萌發或展開的種子。然而，本土註疏家對此有不同的看法。

那些在三摩地（Samadhi）中不超越二十四種本質（Tattvas）、未曾見到第二十五者普魯沙（Purusha）、但至少不再認同身體的人，被稱為無身者（Videhas）；另有一些人尚未見到普魯沙，只見到存在，被稱為體質隱沒者（Prakritilayas），沉浸於體質（Prakriti）之中。這對我來說仍不甚清楚，但我們大可無須深入探討兩種冥想的所有複雜細分，例如有尋論（Savitarka）、有伺察（Savichara）、有喜（Sananda）與有我慢（Sasmita）等。這些細分在更細緻的瑜伽哲學研究中可能顯得重要，但對於思辨哲學家而言，除卻它們提供的一項證據——足以證明在《瑜伽經》（Sutras）正式成書之前，印度已有一段長期延續的瑜伽哲學研究——它們幾難引起興趣。
阿桑普拉亞達三摩地（Asamprajñata-samādhi），或謂無已知對象之瞑想，或可謂無意識之瞑想，乃被解釋為由重複否定知覺所先導，且為一切先前印象之終結。
這條蘇特拉已被歐洲和本土的不同註釋家作過各種不同的解釋。它可能意味著存在著先前印象的殘餘，也可能意味著不存在。三昧迦羅（Samskaras）——我將其譯為「先前印象」——乃是一切賦予心靈其1阿斯米塔（Asmita）不同於阿含卡拉（Ahamkara），其意為我是（Asmi）我並非如此之物——諸如原性（Praknti）、慧（Buddhi）、阿含卡拉、心意（Manas）等等——的誤認。
拉斯瓦巴再論。343 其特殊的性質，可以說其風味或其一般的傾向，「陶罐一旦浸入，將永遠保留其氣味」（Quo semel est imbuta recena servabit odorem Testa diu）。有可能意圖是這些三昧耶（Samskāras）要麼全部被擦除，要麼只剩下極少的殘餘，在停止心的所有功能的最後行為中才得以展現。

在總結了關於三摩地（Samadhi）各種類型的論述之後，帕坦伽利在《瑜伽經》第一卷第19頌中再次指出，在毗提訶人（Videhas）及自性層人（Prakritilayas）的情況下（如前文第342頁所述），三摩地的對象或者說原因是現實世界（Bhava），但對於其他瑜伽士來說，三摩地有其預備條件或步驟，即信、能、念、定、慧依次相承。這些三摩地中的每一種都被仔細地界定了，而在下一頌（I, 21）中還提到了一些進一步的幫助，我們讀到當熱情或意志強烈時，三摩地可以說是近在咫尺或垂手可得的。這些意志堅強或決心堅定的尋求者又根據他們所採用的方式是溫和、中等還是過度而被分類（I, 22）。因此我們得到九類瑜伽士：採用溫和方式者（以溫和、中等或過度的熱情進行），採用中等方式者（以溫和、中等或過度的熱情進行），以及採用過度方式者（以溫和、中等或過度的熱情進行）。
這類的區分與次區分充分證實了僧佉論（Sámkhya）此名—意為列舉—之合理性，使得僧佉哲學與瑜伽哲學都極其繁瑣冗長，我日後將不再贅述這些區分，儘管它們時而可能包含某些有趣的觀察。
> 德文對照：*Ísvara nochmals*
在列舉了學生應當運用的所有瑜伽修習方法之後，接著便是那著名的經句 I, 23，人們向來認為它含有對卡皮拉（Kapila）的回應，以及對於神聖者存在的證明，我之前曾將其譯為「對主的虔誠」。穀物——344 印度哲學。

評論家稱之為單純的權宜之計、一種替代方案。同樣地，與卡延德拉拉爾‧米特拉（Kajendralal Mitra）一樣，將此經文直接譯為「對上帝的虔誠」也是不恰當的。如我們之前所見，伊濕伐羅（Isvara）並非上帝，就如婆羅賀可被稱為上帝的意義而言。他是一位神祇，至高的神祇，但始終是眾多普魯沙（Purusha）中的一位；儘管他被視為聖潔的（I, 25）且全知的，他似乎從未上升至創造者的地位，而在數論哲學（Samkhya）體系中實在沒有此類地位的空間。雖然不容置疑的是，正統瑜伽行者從此經文中獲得了極大的安慰，以此護衛帕坦伽利（Patañjali）免於無神論的指控，但將其視為支持上帝存在的真正證明，或將其視為不僅是相當牽強的信仰告白，實是不可能的。
--- ¹ 原文注號保留供對應德文版本。

由對主的信奉而獲得的利益在本質上並不異於從其他方便法門（Upayas）或達致三摩地（Samadhi）之其他手段所能獲得的利益，如前文所譯的經文第一卷第29至33頌所示。此種信奉甚至也非最後或最高的方便法門，蓋帕坦伽利（Patañjali）隨即在後提及其他同樣有利於心一境性（concentrated meditation）或對一所緣對象思想的吸收的手段。諸如呼吸的驅逐與保持等權便之法隨之而至，即所謂調息法（Prattayamas），我們實可相信此等方法確實曾作為將思想從一切主題轉離，而專注於所選定的冥想對象（通常為諸本質（Tattvas）之一）的工具而發揮作用。但這開啟了對我們此處目的而言過於廣大的主題。我們於此接近瑜伽的病理學部分，即所謂哈達瑜伽（Hatha）或業瑜伽（Kriya-yoga），此誠然是一個遠較一般所謂者為重要的主題，但其為病理學研究者之課題，而非哲學研究者之課題，除非如今已成時尚者般將所謂的生理‧心理學實驗納入哲學之名目。至於我們的經文，至少有一事無可否認：它們對於可藉由身體作用於心靈的紀律之陳述是誠實的；縱使可被證明其在觀察上曾有謬誤，其錯誤觀念於我看來在帕坦伽利的時代似乎並非純然的欺騙，雖然我的目的決非為現代瑜伽行者或大師（Mahatmans）的一切言行進行辯護。

次於調節或制約「呼吸」之後，接著論述心念如何藉由指向鼻尖而認識到天香，以及同樣的情況如何適用於所有其他感官，因此這些感官被認為不再對外在對象有任何傾向，因為它們在自身之內已得到一切所需。我們接著被告知一種內在光明而福樂的知覺狀態的出現，當心念指向那些不再引起熱情的對象時，這種狀態便產生心智的穩定與知足（I, 37）。毫不奇怪地，即便在夢中或睡眠中所見的對象，也被認為能達到同樣的目的，亦即定住注意力。事實上，任何對象都可被選擇作為穩定的冥想對象，譬如外在的月亮或我們內在的心，只要這些對象不引起我們的熱情即可。

這些都是達成目的的手段，毫無疑問它們已經證明了其效力；然而，如同常常發生的情況，這些手段在此例中也明顯地侵佔了目標的地位，而且其程度之深，致使瑜伽（Yoga）經常被理解為在於這些外在的努力，而非在於思想的集中專注——這種集中專注才是這些努力的本意，並且應該引向解脫（Kaivalya）或靈性的分離與自由。這真正的瑜伽往往被區分為王瑜伽（Rāja-yoga）以區別於另一種稱為業瑜伽（Kriya-yoga）或工作瑜伽的種類，有時也稱為哈他瑜伽（Haṭha-yoga），雖然理由並不明確。儘管某些這些身體的修行被表述為如同心靈逐步攀登的階梯，我們在其他時候也聞知任何一步都可能有用，而某些步驟可能被跳過或視為已完成。
現在，若我們問這一切的結果是什麼，《經文》第41頌告訴我們，一個人若已終止心智的一切運動與情感，便會對他感官的一切對象獲得根植於它們之中的一致（sic），或穩定與本質相同，其思想乃是心智實際上被**印度哲學**所改變或轉化。
所知的對象（I, 41）。如同水晶置於紅花旁時，在我們的眼中確實變成紅色，同樣地，心識據說會被所知的對象所染著。這種印象之所以成立，是因為其根植於對象，而我們的心應當始終專注於一個冥想的對象。

在前面的蘇特拉（Sutra）中提及三摩地（Samadhi，此處稱為三摩跋提）可能是有尋（Savitarka）或無尋（Savichara），他現在解釋（第一卷，第42經）當禪定摻雜對於詞語、意義或知識的不確定性時，便稱為有尋（Savitarka）。例如，假設我們的禪定集中於一頭牛，問題便會在於我們應該禪定於「牛」的音聲——梵文 go（गो）——還是它的意義（Begriff），即牛的屬性，或是它所傳達的觀念或心像（Vorstellung）。這樣的禪定稱為有尋（Savitarka）。它的對立面是無尋（Nirvitarka），當一切記憶消散，唯有意義獨存，不帶任何形相之時；或如註釋者所言，雖然表述得不甚清晰，當知識心（Prajna）被其對象之形相所著色，卻遺忘了自身作為知者的主觀形相，並且彷彿在形相上與對象合而為一時。

在描述了三摩地（Samadhi）之後的有分別三摩地（Savitarka）與無分別三摩地（Nirvitarka）之後，下一個分類是有微細對象三摩地（Savi̱tara）與無微細對象三摩地（Nirvi̱tara）。它們被定義為與微細對象有關（I, 44），亦即與本質要素（Tanmatras）、本質與感官有關，由此我們得知前者——有分別三摩地（Savitarka Samadhi）——只須處理物質對象。微細對象包括原質（Prakriti）在內，而在它之外沒有任何微細的東西，因為靈我（Purusha）既非微細亦非非微細。
如果我們將無念三摩地（Nirvicara Samadhi）視為所有三摩地中最高的境界，那麼在完成該冥想之後，將會隨之而來自我（Atman）的滿足或寧靜。在此狀態中的知識被稱為真理含攝（Ritambhara），乃是完全不同於透過推理或啟示所獲得的知識。而從此知識中產生出一種傾向，它克服所有先前的傾向，並使其成為多餘。

卡伊瓦爾塔，自由。347 三摩地（Samādhi） 因此這種知識似乎是真正瑜伽行者的最高目標；但不是這樣，知識之外還有更高的東西，那就是先前所稱的阿普拉吉那塔三摩地（Aprajñāta Samādhi），無對象的冥想，或純粹的狂喜。在瑜伽行者被解脫出生活的一切外在擾動之後，尤其是被解脫出那種造成他暫時將自己等同於原物質（Prakṛti）之任何作為的無明（asmitā）之後，這種狀態將普魯沙（Purusha）恢復到他自身的本性。
凱瓦利亞（Kaivalya），自由。

這篇關於《瑜伽經》第一章所包含內容的簡短敘述，幾乎包含了歐洲哲學家在帕坦伽利（Patañjali）體系中所有可能感興趣的東西，而且這篇文章原本很可能自成一部完整的著作。它向我們展現了瑜伽在其最簡樸形式中的整個旨趣，始於穩定和集中心智於某些事物的方法，尤其是集中於從數論派（Sāṃkhya）所採納的二十四個本質（Tattvas），並進而導向冥想的描述。這種冥想不再受限於任何本質，這相當於一種冥想，不會駐留在任何可透過所謂現實世界的理想表象所提供的事物之上。它實在就是每一個神我（Purusha）對其自身的冥想，作為有別於實性（Prakriti）的所有本質。在真正的瑜伽行者的眼中，這就是獨存（Kaivalya），或最高的福樂；它確實可以被稱為智慧瑜伽（Jñāna-yoga），亦即透過思想或意志單獨進行的瑜伽，的最高成就。外在的輔助手段，如調息法（Prāṇāyāma），即呼吸的吸入和呼出，在此只是略有提及，但這幾乎是對後來時代中變成實踐瑜伽（Kriyā-yoga）最突出部分的唯一提及——即姿態和其他苦行實踐（瑜伽支，Yogaṅgas），這些被認為是為心智自身的更高努力做準備。上文提及的對主宰者的虔誠（Īśvara-praṇidhāna），在此被歸類為瑜伽的簡單配件之一，與淨化、知足、苦行和誦經（《瑜伽經》II, 32）並列，這表明印度哲學中有多麼少的真實性。
